title: Red Snapper Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill stops the government from closing fishing areas in the South Atlantic where people catch red snapper, grouper, and other fish. The government cannot make these closures until they finish a big study that counts red snapper and use that information in their official reports about how many fish are in the ocean. The bill says fishing is worth billions of dollars to people who live near the South Atlantic and that red snapper numbers have gotten better over time. Right now, officials want to close some areas to reduce the number of fish that die when fishermen accidentally catch them and have to throw them back.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Red Snapper Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Conditions for Rules and Secretarial Amendments Related to Certain Area Closures
sections.text: Congress has found the following facts about fishing in the South Atlantic waters. Fishing brings a lot of money to the South Atlantic region. In Florida alone, people who fish for fun bring in fourteen billion dollars to the economy and support one hundred nineteen thousand jobs. Red snapper is a type of fish that both people who fish for fun and people who fish for business really want to catch. In two thousand eighteen, when people were allowed to fish for red snapper for only six days, it added thirteen million dollars to the total economic output of the South Atlantic region. Since two thousand ten, the people who manage fisheries have been working successfully to rebuild the red snapper population in the South Atlantic. Right now, there are more red snapper than ever before and young fish are doing well. This record high number of fish has led to more situations where people catch red snapper when they are not supposed to and have to throw them back, which often kills the fish anyway. Even with these deaths from throwing fish back, the South Atlantic Fishery Management Council's Snapper Grouper Advisory Panel believes that based on their experience fishing on the water, the red snapper fishery has recovered. However, ways to reduce these unwanted catches and deaths of red snapper might include shorter fishing seasons and closing large areas where people fish for snapper and grouper in the South Atlantic. The State of Florida is worried about how closing fishing areas would hurt the economy of recreational snapper and grouper fishing in the South Atlantic. Eight million seven hundred thousand dollars has been spent over the last three budget years on independent survey data, including three million three hundred thousand dollars for the South Atlantic Great Red Snapper Count. This count is meant to estimate how many red snapper live in South Atlantic waters from North Carolina to Florida. The National Marine Fisheries Service should use data from this survey in their fish population assessments as quickly as possible to make better decisions about managing fisheries. The Secretary of Commerce cannot issue any temporary or final rule or Secretarial Amendment that includes closing any area or bottom area in the South Atlantic for fish species managed under the Fishery Management Plan for the Snapper Grouper Fishery of the South Atlantic Region until two things happen. First, the South Atlantic Great Red Snapper Count study must be finished. Second, the information from that study must be included in the first South Atlantic red snapper Southeast Data Assessment and Review stock assessment that happens after this law is passed.
